exemplar.title: To what extent has Coca-Colaâs unethical business practices between the years 2019-2024 impacted its brand image and reputation globally?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent has Coca-Colaâs unethical business practices between the years 2019-2024 impacted its brand image and reputation globally?

subject.title: Business Management
subject.description:
**IB Business Management** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **business fundamentals, human resource management, finance and accounts, marketing, and operations management**, with a strong focus on **case study analysis, real-world business applications, and strategic decision-making**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|----------------|------------|--------|--------|---------------|
| **Paper 1** | Case study-based exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Core themes exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | Social enterprise case study | N/A | 1h 15min | HL: 25% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Written commentary (SL) / Research project (HL) | Various | Various | SL: 30%, HL: 20% |

strengths: Clear identification and definition of Ethics as the key concept
Discussion of three specific ethical issues linked to Coca-Colaâs practices
Strong organizational context anchoring the concept to the company
weaknesses: Ethical lens fades when financial and market analysis begins
Limited integration of ethics framework in later sections
Would benefit from peer-reviewed source to deepen conceptual framing
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the report shows a solid understanding of business ethics and connects it to Coca-Colaâs operations, the application is uneven: strong in the thematic sections but less so in the financial analysis. This aligns with a band-3 performance (basic understanding with simple application) rather than the band-4 level of sustained integration.

strengths: Exactly five relevant supporting documents are used and clearly sign-posted
Range of sources includes company website, investigative article, financial database, brand valuation report, and consumer survey
Transparent methodology paragraph detailing document selection
weaknesses: Consumer survey is very limited in sample size and depth
Documents are used primarily for quotation rather than deeper analytical insight
Lacks critical engagement with the depth and limitations of some sources
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work meets the 3â5 document requirement and uses sources of clear relevance, meriting band-3 (documents relevant and sufficiently in-depth). However, depth of engagement varies and critical analysis of the sources is limited.

strengths: Appropriate selection of Porterâs Generic Strategies and Ansoffâs Matrix
Examples of cost leadership and product development are provided
weaknesses: Limited diversity of analytical tools and theories
Applications are often implicit and not clearly tied to the ethical research question
Superficial treatment of how frameworks relate to risk mitigation and ethical controversies
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student selects and applies some standard business frameworks, but the linkage to the research question on ethics is not consistently explicit and the analysis remains superficialâcharacteristic of a band-2 performance (some tools selected with limited application).
feedback.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

strengths: Uses multiple quantitative and qualitative data sets from 2019â24
Interprets data in relation to brand and reputational impact
Acknowledges methodological limitations (e.g., survey size, U.S.-only market share)
weaknesses: Analysis often remains descriptive rather than deeply evaluative
Some data (e.g., market-share range 1995â2023) fall outside the research window
Limited critical comparison of data sets or integration of ethical dimensions into financial metrics
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis addresses the research question and integrates various data sources, but it lacks the depth, critical evaluation, and sustained insight expected at band-4. This aligns with a band-3 performance (some analysis of the research question).

strengths: Conclusions are fully consistent with the presented evidence
Explicitly answers the research question on ethical impact
Clear and focused summary of key findings
weaknesses: No recommendations or suggestions for further research
Lacks discussion of implications beyond the core findings
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion directly addresses the research question and aligns with the data, fulfilling band-3 expectations (fully consistent conclusions with explicit answer). There is no contradictory material, but the section would benefit from broader implications or recommendations.

strengths: Logical organization with clear headings and table of contents
Appropriate flow from introduction through thematic sections to conclusion
weaknesses: Minor formatting glitches do not impede readability but affect polish
Table of contents could use deeper sub-heading hierarchy for clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The reportâs structure is coherent and easy to follow throughout, meeting the top band-2 descriptor for appropriate structure with only minor issues.

strengths: All required HL IA elements are present (title page, research question, visuals, bibliography, etc.)
Consistent citation style and clear figure captions
weaknesses: Some tables (e.g., annual dividends) have cluttered headers
Occasional layout issues affect visual clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation includes every required element and generally follows formatting standards, warranting band-2 (complete inclusion of required elements), despite minor visual and formatting clutter.

strandSelections.reasoning: The report follows a logical sequence: introduction, thematic sections, conclusion, bibliography, with clear headings and a table of contents.  Minor formatting glitches do not disrupt readability.  This represents âappropriate structure throughoutâ, matching the top band (2 marks) in this criterion.

Each SD is relevant (company website, investigative article, financial database, brand valuation report, small consumer survey).  Depth, however, is mixed: the survey is very limited (25 respondents) and the article/valuation report are used primarily for quotation rather than probing analysis.  Overall relevance is clear and depth is âsufficientâ rather than extensive, corresponding to band 3.

All HL IA elements appear present: title page, research question, table of contents, page numbers, visuals, citations, bibliography and 5 supporting documents.  Therefore the highest presentation band (2 marks) is warranted.
Exactly five supporting documents are sign-posted as SD1âSD5, satisfying the required 3-5 range.  Hence the work fits the 3-mark band for this strand.

Porter and Ansoff are applied to Coca-Cola with examples (e.g., cost leadership via economies of scale, product development via flavour innovations).  The linkage to the research question is sometimes implicit; these sections focus on competitive advantage more than ethical impact, so relevance is adequate but not consistently sharp.  This aligns with band 3: application adequate, relevance not always clear.

The IA draws on multiple quantitative data setsâmarket-share table, brand-value chart, goodwill figures, dividend historyâand a small qualitative survey.  Data span 2019-24, matching the research window, and are integrated in the narrative.  While not exhaustive or critically compared, the selection is wide enough to be considered âsufficientâ, meriting band 4.

The student interprets each data set and consistently links results to the extent of reputational impact, acknowledging limitations such as U.S-only market-share and small survey size.  The analysis is mostly effective though occasionally descriptive.  Ethical issues are tied back to financial and brand metrics, showing reasonable integration but not the sustained depth of the top band.  Hence band 4 is appropriate.
The research question is explicitly revisited in the opening line of the conclusion and an unequivocal answer is provided.  This satisfies band 3 for directly answering the research question.

The student selects two core toolsâPorterâs Generic Strategies and Ansoffâs Matrixâand briefly references the marketing mix.  While limited in variety, these are standard, appropriate frameworks for competitive and growth analysis, meeting the criteria for an adequate selection (band 3).

The conclusion summarises findings that unethical practices have *not* materially harmed Coca-Colaâs brand image, directly mirroring the evidence on market share, brand value, goodwill and consumer survey.  No contradictions with presented data are apparent, so the strand meets band 3 (fully consistent).

The discussion remains tightly focused on Coca-Cola: history, strategy, financials, survey of its consumers, and specific allegations against the firm.  Most arguments and data are anchored directly in the organisationâs context, showing strong analytical connection.  Nonetheless, several explanations (e.g., generic benefits of cost leadership) read formulaically and could apply to any large FMCG firm, so integration is not completely comprehensive.  This aligns with band 4 for strong analysis with partial integration.

The student identifies Ethics as the key concept, offers a working definition, and discusses three specific ethical issues (plastic pollution, green-washing, indirect marketing).  Throughout the report, each major section briefly revisits how these practices might influence brand image or consumer perception, indicating a strong but not seamless integration.  However, once the analysis shifts to market-share and financial data, the ethical lens fades and the narrative becomes largely financial rather than conceptual.  This demonstrates solid understanding with partial, not full, integrationâbest matched to band 4.

annotations.comment: The conclusion heading is explicit and aligns with expectations for a focused final section.
The âIntroductionâ heading establishes clear structure, guiding the reader effectively through the reportâs flow.
The conclusion restates key findings clearly but would benefit from recommendations or areas for further research.
The title page includes all required HL IA elements, such as title, research question, key concept, and visuals, meeting presentation expectations.
The research question is clearly articulated with precise scope and time frame, providing a solid foundation for the investigation.
Labeling the key concept as âEthicsâ is appropriate, but the introduction could integrate an initial definition to frame analysis more effectively.
The table of contents is well structured with clear headings, enhancing navigability throughout the report.
Consider adding subâheadings or indentation levels in the table of contents to reflect the hierarchy of sections more precisely.
The introduction provides strong company context but delays the ethical lens until late; integrate a brief ethics framework earlier.
Porterâs Generic Strategy is correctly introduced, but its relevance to ethical risk mitigation could be made explicit.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The methodology paragraph signposts all five supporting documents, demonstrating adequate selection and transparency of sources.
The initial definition of business ethics is clear but could be reinforced with a peerâreviewed source for depth.
The summary of plastic pollution draws on multiple sources but lacks critical evaluation of how this issue translates into brand impact.
The greenwashing section presents goal revisions clearly; consider contrasting these with external benchmarks for deeper insight.
The marketing practices described are relevant, but the link back to ethical implications in the research question is underdeveloped.
The discussion of misleading nutrition claims is informative but remains descriptive; add evaluative commentary on reputational effects.
The application of cost leadership is well explained with concrete examples, demonstrating adequate analytical use of Porterâs framework.
The inclusion of Figure 2âs caption follows presentation standards, identifying the framework clearly.
Ansoffâs Matrix is introduced but application is superficial; add deeper explanation of risk implications for ethical controversies.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The differentiation strategy section provides rich examples, though linking these to reputational resilience against ethical scandals would enhance relevance.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: Figure 3 presents useful marketâshare data; include a brief discussion on global versus U.S. trends for comprehensive analysis.
The âBrand Valueâ section clearly reports a rise from 2019 to 2024, effectively linking evidence to the research question.
The brandâvalue chart is informative but would benefit from highlighting specific years where ethical controversies occurred.
The goodwill table spans a long period; focus commentary on 2019â2024 trends to support the research question.
The narrative asserts that market share remains unaffected, but data spans 1995â2023, outside the 2019â2024 window; align data to the research period.
The narrative connecting dividend growth to financial strength is effective; consider discussing ethical investment appeal.
The consumer perception section clearly identifies survey findings but should critique sample representativeness more rigorously.
Citing Brand Finance for sustainability perceptions is relevant, but critique its methodology to deepen evaluative insight.
The annual dividends table contains cluttered headers that impede readability; streamline formatting to clarify key figures.
The detailed survey narrative conveys consumer attitudes, yet analytic depth is limited by lack of demographic breakdown.
